Figure 114 Sparse MMA metadata example for `.u8`/`.s8` type.

when matrices A and B have `.u4`/`.s4` elements, matrix A is pair-wise structured sparse at a
granularity of 4:8. In other words, each chunk of eight adjacent elements in a row of matrix A has
four zeroes and four non-zero values. Further, the zero and non-zero values are clustered in
sub-chunks of two elements each within the eight-wide chunk. i.e., each two-wide sub-chunk within
the eight-wide chunk must be all zeroes or all non-zeros. Only the four non-zero values are stored
in sparse matrix and the positions of the two two-wide sub-chunks with non-zero values in the
eight-wide chunk of a row of matrix A are indicated by two 2-bit indices in the metadata. For
`mma.sp::ordered_metadata`, `0b0100`, `0b1000`, `0b1001`, `0b1100`, `0b1101`, `0b1110`
are the meaningful values of indices; any other values result in an undefined behavior.





[Figure: u4s4-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .u4/.s4 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates how structured sparsity metadata is encoded for 4-bit integer types (u4/s4) in sparse MMA operations. It shows the relationship between an eight-wide chunk of matrix A, the compressed sparse operand, and the metadata encoding.

============================================================
LEFT SIDE: EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
============================================================

This is a 32-bit register containing 8 elements of 4-bit type (8 x 4 bits = 32 bits).

Bit layout (bits 31 down to 0):

Bits 31-28: element z
Bits 27-24: element y
Bits 23-20: value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 19-16: value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 15-12: value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 11-8:  value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 7-4:   element x
Bits 3-0:   element w

Visual representation (left=MSB, right=LSB):
[z | y | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | x | w]
 31                        7 3 0

The bit position markers shown are: 31 (leftmost), 7 (start of x), 3 (start of w), 0 (end).

Only 4 of the 8 elements are non-zero (w, x, y, z), representing 2:4 structured sparsity (2 non-zero out of every 4 elements). In an 8-wide chunk this gives 4 non-zero out of 8.

============================================================
LEFT SIDE: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK
============================================================

This is a 32-bit value encoding the 2-bit indices of the four non-zero elements within the 8-wide chunk.

Bit layout (bits 31 down to 0):

Bits 31-30: 11  (index of element z = position 7 in 0-based from right, but encoded as 11 binary = 3 decimal... representing the index within a group of 4)
Bits 29-28: (part of 11 field)
Bits 27-24: 10  (index of element y = binary 10 = decimal 2... wait, each field is 2 bits)

More precisely, the 32-bit index register contains four 2-bit index fields (one per non-zero element):

Bits 31-24: 11  (2-bit index for element z, value = 3)
Bits 23-16: 10  (2-bit index for element y, value = 2... but shown as occupying equal quarters)

Actually from the figure, the 32-bit field is divided into four equal sections of 8 bits each, but the labels show 2-bit values:

Section bits 31-24: label "11"
Section bits 23-16: label "10"
Section bits 15-8:  label "01"
Section bits 7-0:   label "00"

Bit range markers: 31 (leftmost), 0 (rightmost)

These are the 2-bit indices of the four non-zero elements (w, x, y, z) within the 8-wide chunk:
- Element w is at index 00 (position 0)
- Element x is at index 01 (position 1)
- Element y is at index 10 (position 2... within second group of 4)
- Element z is at index 11 (position 3... within second group of 4)

Wait — re-reading: the indices 00, 01, 10, 11 are stored from right to left (LSB to MSB):
Bits 7-0:   00 (index for w)
Bits 15-8:  01 (index for x)
Bits 23-16: 10 (index for y)
Bits 31-24: 11 (index for z)

============================================================
RIGHT SIDE: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
============================================================

This is a 16-bit compressed register containing only the 4 non-zero elements (4 x 4 bits = 16 bits).

Bit layout (bits 15 down to 0):

Bits 15-12: element z
Bits 11-8:  element y
Bits 7-4:   element x
Bits 3-0:   element w

Visual representation:
[z | y | x | w]
 15           0

The four zero elements have been removed; only w, x, y, z are stored in the compressed operand.

============================================================
RIGHT SIDE: METADATA
============================================================

This is a 4-bit metadata value encoding the positions of the non-zero elements.

Bit layout (bits 3 down to 0):

Bits 3-2: 11  (2-bit index for the second non-zero pair, value = 3 decimal)
Bits 1-0: 00  (2-bit index for the first non-zero pair, value = 0 decimal)

Bit range markers: 3 (leftmost), 1 (middle boundary), 0 (rightmost)

Visual representation:
[11 | 00]
  3  1  0

============================================================
ENCODING INTERPRETATION
============================================================

For .u4/.s4 type with 2:4 sparsity in an 8-wide chunk:

The metadata is 4 bits total, divided into two 2-bit fields:

Field bits 1-0 = 00: indicates the first non-zero element (w) is at position 0 within the lower group of 4 elements (positions 0-3 of the 8-wide chunk).

Field bits 3-2 = 11: indicates the second non-zero element (x) is at position 3 within the lower group of 4 elements... 

More precisely: the 8-wide chunk is divided into two groups of 4. Within each group of 4, exactly 2 elements are non-zero (2:4 sparsity). Each 2-bit metadata field encodes which 2 of the 4 positions are non-zero within that group.

The metadata value 00 for bits 1-0 selects position 0 (element w at index 0 of lower group).
The metadata value 11 for bits 3-2 selects position 3 (element z at index 3 of upper group).

Actually the figure shows only 4 bits of metadata for the entire 8-wide chunk, with:
- 2 bits (bits 1-0) = 00: encodes index of first selected element = position 0 (w)
- 2 bits (bits 3-2) = 11: encodes index of second selected element = position 3 (z) within the second half

The non-zero elements selected are w (position 0), x (position 1), y (position 6 or equivalent), z (position 7 or equivalent) based on the original chunk layout.

============================================================
SUMMARY OF CORRESPONDENCE
============================================================

Original 8-wide chunk positions (0=rightmost/LSB):
Position 0: w (non-zero)
Position 1: x (non-zero)
Position 2: 0 (zero)
Position 3: 0 (zero)
Position 4: 0 (zero)
Position 5: 0 (zero)
Position 6: y (non-zero)
Position 7: z (non-zero)

Compressed sparse operand (16-bit): [z, y, x, w] at bits [15:12, 11:8, 7:4, 3:0]

Metadata (4-bit): bits[3:2]=11, bits[1:0]=00
- bits[1:0]=00 encodes that within the first group of 4 (positions 0-3), the non-zero elements are at sub-positions 0 and 1 (w and x)
- bits[3:2]=11 encodes that within the second group of 4 (positions 4-7), the non-zero elements are at sub-positions 2 and 3 (y and z)

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates how structured sparsity metadata is encoded for 4-bit integer types (u4/s4) in sparse MMA operations. It shows the relationship between an eight-wide chunk of matrix A, the compressed sparse operand, and the metadata encoding.

============================================================
LEFT SIDE: EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
============================================================

This is a 32-bit register containing 8 elements of 4-bit type (8 x 4 bits = 32 bits).

Bit layout (bits 31 down to 0):

Bits 31-28: element z
Bits 27-24: element y
Bits 23-20: value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 19-16: value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 15-12: value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 11-8:  value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 7-4:   element x
Bits 3-0:   element w

Visual representation (left=MSB, right=LSB):
[z | y | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | x | w]
 31                        7 3 0

The bit position markers shown are: 31 (leftmost), 7 (start of x), 3 (start of w), 0 (end).

Only 4 of the 8 elements are non-zero (w, x, y, z), representing 2:4 structured sparsity (2 non-zero out of every 4 elements). In an 8-wide chunk this gives 4 non-zero out of 8.

============================================================
LEFT SIDE: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK
============================================================

This is a 32-bit value encoding the 2-bit indices of the four non-zero elements within the 8-wide chunk.

Bit layout (bits 31 down to 0):

Bits 31-30: 11  (index of element z = position 7 in 0-based from right, but encoded as 11 binary = 3 decimal... representing the index within a group of 4)
Bits 29-28: (part of 11 field)
Bits 27-24: 10  (index of element y = binary 10 = decimal 2... wait, each field is 2 bits)

More precisely, the 32-bit index register contains four 2-bit index fields (one per non-zero element):

Bits 31-24: 11  (2-bit index for element z, value = 3)
Bits 23-16: 10  (2-bit index for element y, value = 2... but shown as occupying equal quarters)

Actually from the figure, the 32-bit field is divided into four equal sections of 8 bits each, but the labels show 2-bit values:

Section bits 31-24: label "11"
Section bits 23-16: label "10"
Section bits 15-8:  label "01"
Section bits 7-0:   label "00"

Bit range markers: 31 (leftmost), 0 (rightmost)

These are the 2-bit indices of the four non-zero elements (w, x, y, z) within the 8-wide chunk:
- Element w is at index 00 (position 0)
- Element x is at index 01 (position 1)
- Element y is at index 10 (position 2... within second group of 4)
- Element z is at index 11 (position 3... within second group of 4)

Wait — re-reading: the indices 00, 01, 10, 11 are stored from right to left (LSB to MSB):
Bits 7-0:   00 (index for w)
Bits 15-8:  01 (index for x)
Bits 23-16: 10 (index for y)
Bits 31-24: 11 (index for z)

============================================================
RIGHT SIDE: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
============================================================

This is a 16-bit compressed register containing only the 4 non-zero elements (4 x 4 bits = 16 bits).

Bit layout (bits 15 down to 0):

Bits 15-12: element z
Bits 11-8:  element y
Bits 7-4:   element x
Bits 3-0:   element w

Visual representation:
[z | y | x | w]
 15           0

The four zero elements have been removed; only w, x, y, z are stored in the compressed operand.

============================================================
RIGHT SIDE: METADATA
============================================================

This is a 4-bit metadata value encoding the positions of the non-zero elements.

Bit layout (bits 3 down to 0):

Bits 3-2: 11  (2-bit index for the second non-zero pair, value = 3 decimal)
Bits 1-0: 00  (2-bit index for the first non-zero pair, value = 0 decimal)

Bit range markers: 3 (leftmost), 1 (middle boundary), 0 (rightmost)

Visual representation:
[11 | 00]
  3  1  0

============================================================
ENCODING INTERPRETATION
============================================================

For .u4/.s4 type with 2:4 sparsity in an 8-wide chunk:

The metadata is 4 bits total, divided into two 2-bit fields:

Field bits 1-0 = 00: indicates the first non-zero element (w) is at position 0 within the lower group of 4 elements (positions 0-3 of the 8-wide chunk).

Field bits 3-2 = 11: indicates the second non-zero element (x) is at position 3 within the lower group of 4 elements... 

More precisely: the 8-wide chunk is divided into two groups of 4. Within each group of 4, exactly 2 elements are non-zero (2:4 sparsity). Each 2-bit metadata field encodes which 2 of the 4 positions are non-zero within that group.

The metadata value 00 for bits 1-0 selects position 0 (element w at index 0 of lower group).
The metadata value 11 for bits 3-2 selects position 3 (element z at index 3 of upper group).

Actually the figure shows only 4 bits of metadata for the entire 8-wide chunk, with:
- 2 bits (bits 1-0) = 00: encodes index of first selected element = position 0 (w)
- 2 bits (bits 3-2) = 11: encodes index of second selected element = position 3 (z) within the second half

The non-zero elements selected are w (position 0), x (position 1), y (position 6 or equivalent), z (position 7 or equivalent) based on the original chunk layout.

============================================================
SUMMARY OF CORRESPONDENCE
============================================================

Original 8-wide chunk positions (0=rightmost/LSB):
Position 0: w (non-zero)
Position 1: x (non-zero)
Position 2: 0 (zero)
Position 3: 0 (zero)
Position 4: 0 (zero)
Position 5: 0 (zero)
Position 6: y (non-zero)
Position 7: z (non-zero)

Compressed sparse operand (16-bit): [z, y, x, w] at bits [15:12, 11:8, 7:4, 3:0]

Metadata (4-bit): bits[3:2]=11, bits[1:0]=00
- bits[1:0]=00 encodes that within the first group of 4 (positions 0-3), the non-zero elements are at sub-positions 0 and 1 (w and x)
- bits[3:2]=11 encodes that within the second group of 4 (positions 4-7), the non-zero elements are at sub-positions 2 and 3 (y and z)


Figure 115 Sparse MMA metadata example for `.u4`/`.s4` type.

The sparsity selector indicates the threads which contribute metadata as listed below:

* `m16n8k32` with `.u8`/`.s8` type and `m16n8k64` with `.u4`/`.s4` type: A thread-pair
  within a group of four consecutive threads contributes the sparsity metadata. Hence, the sparsity
  selector must be either 0 (threads T0, T1) or 1 (threads T2, T3); any other value results in an
  undefined behavior.
* `m16n8k64` with `.u8`/`.s8` type and `m16n8k128` with `.u4`/`.s4` type: All threads
  within a group of four consecutive threads contribute the sparsity metadata. Hence, the sparsity
  selector in this case must be 0. Any other value of sparsity selector results in an undefined
  behavior.

Sparse `mma.sp{::ordered_metadata}` operating on `.e4m3`/`.e5m2`/`.e3m2`/`.e2m3`/`.e2m1`
type with `.kind::f8f6f4` or `.kind::mxf8f6f4`

When matrices A and B have `.e4m3`/`.e5m2`/`.e3m2`/`.e2m3`/`.e2m1` elements, matrix A is
structured sparse at a granularity of 2:4. In other words, each chunk of four adjacent elements in a
row of matrix A have two zeroes and two non-zero elements. Only the two non-zero elements are stored
in sparse matrix and their positions in the four-wide chunk are indicated by two 2-bit indices in the
metadata. `0b0100`, `0b1000`, `0b1001`, `0b1100`, `0b1101`, `0b1110` are the meaningful
values of indices; any other values result in an undefined behavior.





[Figure: fp8-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .e4m3/.e5m2/.e3m2/.e2m3/.e2m1 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the relationship between a four-wide chunk from matrix A, the sparse matrix operand (compressed), and the metadata encoding used to identify which elements are non-zero.

============================================================
SECTION 1: FOUR-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
============================================================
This is a 32-bit register (bits 31 down to 0) containing four elements arranged as follows:

Bit range: 31 ............................................. 0
+----------+----------+----------+----------+
|    y     |    0     |    0     |    x     |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+
  bits[31:24] bits[23:16] bits[15:8]  bits[7:0]

- Position 3 (bits 31:24): element y (non-zero)
- Position 2 (bits 23:16): element 0 (zero)
- Position 1 (bits 15:8):  element 0 (zero)
- Position 0 (bits 7:0):   element x (non-zero)

The four elements occupy a 32-bit word. Two elements are non-zero (x and y), two are zero. This represents 2:4 structured sparsity (2 non-zero values out of every 4).

============================================================
SECTION 2: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE FOUR-WIDE CHUNK
============================================================
This is a 32-bit word (bits 31 down to 0) showing the 2-bit index assigned to each of the four positions:

Bit range: 31 ............................................. 0
+----------+----------+----------+----------+
|    11    |    10    |    01    |    00    |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+
  bits[31:30] bits[29:28] bits[27:26] bits[25:24]
  (index=3)   (index=2)   (index=1)   (index=0)

Each element position in the four-wide chunk is assigned a 2-bit binary index:
- Position 3: index 11 (binary) = 3 (decimal)
- Position 2: index 10 (binary) = 2 (decimal)
- Position 1: index 01 (binary) = 1 (decimal)
- Position 0: index 00 (binary) = 0 (decimal)

============================================================
SECTION 3: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND (COMPRESSED)
============================================================
This is a 16-bit register (bits 15 down to 0) containing only the two non-zero elements packed together:

Bit range: 15 ......................... 0
+----------+----------+
|    y     |    x     |
+----------+----------+
  bits[15:8]  bits[7:0]

- Upper half (bits 15:8): element y (the non-zero element from position 3)
- Lower half (bits 7:0):  element x (the non-zero element from position 0)

The two zero elements are dropped; only x and y are stored in the compressed sparse operand.

============================================================
SECTION 4: METADATA
============================================================
This is a 4-bit metadata field (bits 3 down to 0) encoding the indices of the two non-zero elements:

Bit range: 3 ........... 0
+----------+----------+
|    11    |    00    |
+----------+----------+
  bits[3:2]   bits[1:0]

- bits[3:2] = 11 (binary): index of the second non-zero element (y), which was at position 3 in the four-wide chunk
- bits[1:0] = 00 (binary): index of the first non-zero element (x), which was at position 0 in the four-wide chunk

METADATA ENCODING INTERPRETATION:
- Each non-zero element in the compressed sparse operand is associated with a 2-bit index field in the metadata.
- The first stored non-zero element (x, stored in bits[7:0] of the sparse operand) has its original position encoded in metadata bits[1:0] = 00, meaning it came from position 0 of the four-wide chunk.
- The second stored non-zero element (y, stored in bits[15:8] of the sparse operand) has its original position encoded in metadata bits[3:2] = 11, meaning it came from position 3 of the four-wide chunk.

============================================================
SUMMARY OF ENCODING RELATIONSHIP
============================================================
Four-wide chunk position:  3(y)   2(0)   1(0)   0(x)
                           |                     |
                           |                     |
Sparse operand (16-bit):   y (bits 15:8)    x (bits 7:0)
                           |                     |
Metadata (4-bit):          11 (bits 3:2)    00 (bits 1:0)

The metadata bits directly encode the original column indices (within the four-wide chunk) of the two non-zero values stored in the compressed sparse operand. This allows the hardware to reconstruct which positions in the full matrix row the non-zero values correspond to, enabling correct sparse matrix multiplication.

Figure 116 Sparse MMA metadata example for `.e4m3`/`.e5m2`/`.e3m2`/`.e2m3`/`.e2m1` type.

The sparsity selector indicates the threads which contribute metadata as listed below:

* `m16n8k64`: All threads within a group of four consecutive threads contribute the sparsity metadata.
  Hence, the sparsity selector in this case must be 0. Any other value of sparsity selector results in
  an undefined behavior.

Sparse `mma.sp::ordered_metadata` operating on `.e2m1` type with `.kind::mxf4` or `.kind::mxf4nvf4`

When matrices A and B have `.e2m1` elements, matrix A is pair-wise structured sparse at a granularity
of 4:8. In other words, each chunk of eight adjacent elements in a row of matrix A has four zeroes and
four non-zero values. Further, the zero and non-zero values are clustered in sub-chunks of two elements
each within the eight-wide chunk. i.e., each two-wide sub-chunk within the eight-wide chunk must be all
zeroes or all non-zeros. Only the four non-zero values are stored in sparse matrix and the positions of
the two two-wide sub-chunks with non-zero values in the eight-wide chunk of a row of matrix A are
indicated by two 2-bit indices in the metadata. `0b0100`, `0b1000`, `0b1001`, `0b1100`, `0b1101`,
`0b1110` are the meaningful values of indices; any other values result in an undefined behavior.





[Figure: fp4-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .e2m1 TYPE WITH .kind::mxf4 OR .kind::mxf4nvf4

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates how structured sparsity (2:4 sparsity pattern) metadata is encoded for the e2m1 (MX FP4) data type in sparse MMA operations. It shows the relationship between an eight-wide chunk of matrix A, the compressed sparse operand, and the metadata encoding.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
LEFT SIDE: EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bit range: bits 31 down to 0 (32-bit wide representation)
This represents 8 elements from a row of matrix A, where each element occupies 4 bits (e2m1 format).

Layout (left=MSB, right=LSB):
  Bit positions:  31    27    23    19    15    11    7     3    0
  Elements:       [ z ][ y ][ 0 ][ 0 ][ 0 ][ 0 ][ x ][ w ]

Specific bit boundaries labeled in figure:
  - Bit 31: left edge (MSB)
  - Bit 7: boundary between zero-elements and element x
  - Bit 3: boundary between element x and element w
  - Bit 0: right edge (LSB)

Element positions within the 8-wide chunk (indexed 0 through 7, right to left):
  Index 0: w  (bits 3:0)
  Index 1: x  (bits 7:4)
  Index 2: 0  (bits 11:8)   -- zero (pruned)
  Index 3: 0  (bits 15:12)  -- zero (pruned)
  Index 4: 0  (bits 19:16)  -- zero (pruned)
  Index 5: 0  (bits 23:20)  -- zero (pruned)
  Index 6: y  (bits 27:24)
  Index 7: z  (bits 31:28)

Non-zero elements: w (index 00), x (index 01), y (index 10), z (index 11)
Zero elements: indices 02, 03, 04, 05 (four zeros out of eight = 4:8 sparsity, equivalent to 2:4 at the nibble level)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
LEFT SIDE: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bit range: bits 31 down to 0 (32-bit wide representation)
This shows the 2-bit index values for each of the 4 non-zero elements.

Layout (left=MSB, right=LSB):
  Bit positions:  31         23         15         7        0
  Index values:  [ 11 ]    [ 10 ]    [ 01 ]    [ 00 ]

  Bits 31:24 = 11  (index of element z within the 8-wide chunk)
  Bits 23:16 = 10  (index of element y within the 8-wide chunk)
  Bits 15:8  = 01  (index of element x within the 8-wide chunk)
  Bits 7:0   = 00  (index of element w within the 8-wide chunk)

Note: Each index field is 8 bits wide in this representation, but only the 2 LSBs of each field are meaningful as the actual element index (values 00, 01, 10, 11).

------------------------------------------------------------------------
RIGHT SIDE: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bit range: bits 15 down to 0 (16-bit wide representation)
This is the compressed operand containing only the 4 non-zero elements.

Layout (left=MSB, right=LSB):
  Bit positions:  15    11    7     3    0
  Elements:       [ z ][ y ][ x ][ w ]

  Bits 15:12 = z  (4-bit e2m1 value)
  Bits 11:8  = y  (4-bit e2m1 value)
  Bits 7:4   = x  (4-bit e2m1 value)
  Bits 3:0   = w  (4-bit e2m1 value)

The four non-zero values w, x, y, z are packed contiguously into a 16-bit word, with zeros removed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
RIGHT SIDE: METADATA
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bit range: bits 3 down to 0 (4-bit wide representation)
This is the metadata encoding that records which elements from the original 8-wide chunk are non-zero.

Layout (left=MSB, right=LSB):
  Bit positions:  3    2    1    0
  Values:        [ 11 ]   [ 00 ]

  Bits 3:2 = 11  (2-bit index: identifies element z/y pair position in original chunk)
  Bits 1:0 = 00  (2-bit index: identifies element x/w pair position in original chunk)

Bit boundary labels:
  Bit 3: left edge (MSB)
  Bit 1: boundary between the two 2-bit metadata fields
  Bit 0: right edge (LSB)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
ENCODING INTERPRETATION:
------------------------------------------------------------------------
The metadata is 4 bits total, split into two 2-bit fields:

  Metadata bits 1:0 = 00 -> the first pair of non-zero elements (w, x) are located at positions 0 and 1 within the 8-wide chunk (i.e., the lowest two indices)
  Metadata bits 3:2 = 11 -> the second pair of non-zero elements (y, z) are located at positions 6 and 7 within the 8-wide chunk (i.e., the highest two indices)

The 2-bit metadata field encodes which group-of-two within the 8-wide chunk contains the non-zero pair:
  00 = positions 0,1
  01 = positions 2,3
  10 = positions 4,5
  11 = positions 6,7

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SPARSITY PATTERN SUMMARY:
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Original 8-wide chunk:  [ z | y | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | x | w ]  (indices 7..0)
Non-zero positions:     indices 0, 1, 6, 7
Zero positions:         indices 2, 3, 4, 5
Compressed operand:     [ z | y | x | w ] (16 bits, 4 x 4-bit e2m1 elements)
Metadata:               4 bits: [11][00] (bits 3:2 = 11, bits 1:0 = 00)

This represents a 4:8 structured sparsity pattern (4 non-zeros out of every 8 elements), encoded as two 2-bit selectors in the metadata register, each selector identifying which pair-of-two positions within the 8-wide chunk holds the non-zero values.

Non-zero positions:     indices 0, 1, 6, 7
Zero positions:         indices 2, 3, 4, 5
Compressed operand:     [ z | y | x | w ] (16 bits, 4 x 4-bit e2m1 elements)
Metadata:               4 bits: [11][00] (bits 3:2 = 11, bits 1:0 = 00)

This represents a 4:8 structured sparsity pattern (4 non-zeros out of every 8 elements), encoded as two 2-bit selectors in the metadata register, each selector identifying which pair-of-two positions within the 8-wide chunk holds the non-zero values.


Figure 117 Sparse MMA metadata example for `.e2m1` type with `.kind::mxf4` or `.kind::mxf4nvf4`

The sparsity selector indicates the threads which contribute metadata as listed below:

* `m16n8k128`: All threads within a group of four consecutive threads contribute the sparsity metadata.
  Hence, the sparsity selector in this case must be 0. Any other value of sparsity selector results in
  an undefined behavior.

##### 9.7.14.6.2. [Matrix fragments for multiply-accumulate operation with sparse matrix A](#warp-level-matrix-fragments-for-sparse-mma)

In this section we describe how the contents of thread registers are associated with fragments of
various matrices and the sparsity metadata. The following conventions are used throughout this
section:

* For matrix A, only the layout of a fragment is described in terms of register vector sizes and
  their association with the matrix data.
* For matrix B, when the combination of matrix dimension and the supported data type is not already
  covered in [Matrix multiply-accumulate operation using mma instruction](#warp-level-matrix-instructions-for-mma), a pictorial representation of matrix
  fragments is provided.
* For matrices C and D, since the matrix dimension - data type combination is the same for all
  supported shapes, and is already covered in
  [Matrix multiply-accumulate operation using mma instruction](#warp-level-matrix-instructions-for-mma), the pictorial representations
  of matrix fragments are not included in this section.
* For the metadata operand, pictorial representations of the association between indices of the
  elements of matrix A and the contents of the metadata operand are included. `Tk: [m..n]` present
  in cell `[x][y..z]` indicates that bits `m` through `n` (with `m` being higher) in the
  metadata operand of thread with `%laneid=k` contains the indices of the non-zero elements from
  the chunk `[x][y]..[x][z]` of matrix A.

###### 9.7.14.6.2.1. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k16` with `.f16` and `.bf16` types](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-16816-f16bf16)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k16` with `.f16` / `.bf16` floating point type will compute
an MMA operation of shape `.m16n8k16`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.f16` / `.bf16` | A vector expression containing two `.b32` registers, with each register containing two non-zero `.f16` / `.bf16` elements out of 4 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 118](#sparse-mma-16816-f16-bf16-a).

=============================================================
This is a SPARSE MMA layout. The K dimension is nominally 16, but the sparse
format means only 8 non-zero values per row are stored (2:4 sparsity).
Each thread stores 4 f16/bf16 values (a0, a1, a2, a3) representing the
non-zero elements from two rows of the sparse A matrix.
The 4 columns per band (e.g., cols 0-3) reflect the compressed sparse storage
where 4 columns of data are held per thread position.

format means only 8 non-zero values per row are stored (2:4 sparsity).

The 4 columns per band (e.g., cols 0-3) reflect the compressed sparse storage
where 4 columns of data are held per thread position.


  Figure 118 Sparse MMA .m16n8k16 fragment layout for matrix A with `.f16`/`.bf16` type.

  The row and column of a matrix fragment can be computed as:

  ```
  groupID = %laneid >> 2
  threadID_in_group = %laneid % 4

  row =      groupID            for a0 and a1
             groupID + 8        for a2 and a3

  col = [firstcol ... lastcol]  // As per the mapping of non-zero elements
                                // as described in Sparse matrix storage

  Where
  firstcol = threadID_in_group * 4
  lastcol  = firstcol + 3
  ```
* Matrix fragments for multiplicand B and accumulators C and D are the same as in case of
  [Matrix Fragments for mma.m16n8k16 with floating point type](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-mma-16816-float) for `.f16`/`.b16` formats.
* Metadata: A `.b32` register containing 16 2-bit vectors each storing the index of a non-zero
  element of a 4-wide chunk of matrix A as shown in [Figure 119](#sparse-mma-metadata-16816-f16bf16).

  >

==============================================================
1. MMA SHAPE AND DATA TYPES
==============================================================
Shape: m16n8k16
Data types: .f16 / .bf16
This is a 2:4 structured sparsity metadata layout.
The matrix A has 16 rows and 16 columns (k=16 in the dense sense, k=8 in compressed sense).

==============================================================
2. FULL TABLE: THREAD AND BIT ASSIGNMENTS PER ROW AND COLUMN GROUP
==============================================================

The table is indexed by Row (0-15) and Column (0-15).
Columns are grouped into four groups of 4: [0-3], [4-7], [8-11], [12-15].

Row \ Column Group   | Cols 0-3          | Cols 4-7          | Cols 8-11         | Cols 12-15
---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------
Row 0                | T_i:    [3..0]    | T_i:    [7..4]    | T_i:    [11..8]   | T_i:    [15..12]
Row 1                | T_i+4:  [3..0]    | T_i+4:  [7..4]    | T_i+4:  [11..8]   | T_i+4:  [15..12]
Row 2                | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern)
...                  | ...               | ...               | ...               | ...
Row 7                | T_i+28: [3..0]    | T_i+28: [7..4]    | T_i+28: [11..8]   | T_i+28: [15..12]
---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------
Row 8                | T_i:    [19..16]  | T_i:    [23..20]  | T_i:    [27..24]  | T_i:    [31..28]
Row 9                | T_i+4:  [19..16]  | T_i+4:  [23..20]  | T_i+4:  [27..24]  | T_i+4:  [31..28]
Row 10               | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern)
...                  | ...               | ...               | ...               | ...
Row 15               | T_i+28: [19..16]  | T_i+28: [23..20]  | T_i+28: [27..24]  | T_i+28: [31..28]

==============================================================
3. METADATA ENCODING FORMAT — BIT POSITIONS
==============================================================

Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register. The bits are divided into 4-bit fields, each field encoding the sparsity selector for one group of 4 columns within a row.

For rows 0-7 (upper half of the 16-row matrix):
  Bits [3..0]   -> column group 0-3
  Bits [7..4]   -> column group 4-7
  Bits [11..8]  -> column group 8-11
  Bits [15..12] -> column group 12-15

For rows 8-15 (lower half of the 16-row matrix):
  Bits [19..16] -> column group 0-3
  Bits [23..20] -> column group 4-7
  Bits [27..24] -> column group 8-11
  Bits [31..28] -> column group 12-15

Each 4-bit field encodes two 2-bit selectors (one per non-zero element in a 2:4 sparse group):
  Bits [1..0] of the 4-bit field: selector for first non-zero element (which of 4 positions it occupies)
  Bits [3..2] of the 4-bit field: selector for second non-zero element (which of 4 positions it occupies)

==============================================================
4. THREAD STRIDE PATTERN
==============================================================

The base thread index is T_i (where i is the starting thread in a warp group).
Rows within each half (rows 0-7 and rows 8-15) are assigned to threads with stride +4:

  Row 0  -> T_i      (bits [15..0] for cols 0-15)
  Row 1  -> T_i+4    (bits [15..0] for cols 0-15)
  Row 2  -> T_i+8    (implied)
  Row 3  -> T_i+12   (implied)
  Row 4  -> T_i+16   (implied)
  Row 5  -> T_i+20   (implied)
  Row 6  -> T_i+24   (implied)
  Row 7  -> T_i+28   (bits [15..0] for cols 0-15)

  Row 8  -> T_i      (bits [31..16] for cols 0-15)
  Row 9  -> T_i+4    (bits [31..16] for cols 0-15)
  Row 10 -> T_i+8    (implied)
  Row 11 -> T_i+12   (implied)
  Row 12 -> T_i+16   (implied)
  Row 13 -> T_i+20   (implied)
  Row 14 -> T_i+24   (implied)
  Row 15 -> T_i+28   (bits [31..16] for cols 0-15)

The same thread T_i holds metadata for both row 0 (in bits [15..0]) and row 8 (in bits [31..16]).
The same thread T_i+4 holds metadata for both row 1 (in bits [15..0]) and row 9 (in bits [31..16]).
This pattern continues through T_i+28 covering rows 7 and 15.

==============================================================
5. COLUMN/ROW GROUPING PATTERNS
==============================================================

Column grouping: Columns are grouped in sets of 4 (cols 0-3, 4-7, 8-11, 12-15).
Each group of 4 columns corresponds to one 4-bit metadata field.
Within each 4-column group, the 2:4 sparsity rule applies: exactly 2 of the 4 elements are non-zero, and the 4-bit field encodes which 2 positions are selected.

Row grouping: Rows 0-7 form the upper half; rows 8-15 form the lower half.
The upper half uses the lower 16 bits of the thread's metadata register.
The lower half uses the upper 16 bits of the thread's metadata register.

Thread stride within each half is 4 (T_i, T_i+4, T_i+8, ..., T_i+28), covering 8 rows per half using 8 threads spaced 4 apart in thread index.

The arrows visible in the figure indicate the continuation of the pattern from rows 1/9 through rows 7/15, confirming the stride-4 thread assignment fills in the omitted intermediate rows (2-6 and 10-14).

  Bits [31..28] -> column group 12-15

Each 4-bit field encodes two 2-bit selectors (one per non-zero element in a 2:4 sparse group):
  Bits [1..0] of the 4-bit field: selector for first non-zero element (which of 4 positions it occupies)
  Bits [3..2] of the 4-bit field: selector for second non-zero element (which of 4 positions it occupies)

==============================================================
4. THREAD STRIDE PATTERN
==============================================================

The base thread index is T_i (where i is the starting thread in a warp group).
Rows within each half (rows 0-7 and rows 8-15) are assigned to threads with stride +4:

  Row 0  -> T_i      (bits [15..0] for cols 0-15)
  Row 1  -> T_i+4    (bits [15..0] for cols 0-15)
  Row 2  -> T_i+8    (implied)
  Row 3  -> T_i+12   (implied)
  Row 4  -> T_i+16   (implied)
  Row 5  -> T_i+20   (implied)
  Row 6  -> T_i+24   (implied)
  Row 7  -> T_i+28   (bits [15..0] for cols 0-15)

  Row 8  -> T_i      (bits [31..16] for cols 0-15)
  Row 9  -> T_i+4    (bits [31..16] for cols 0-15)
  Row 10 -> T_i+8    (implied)
  Row 11 -> T_i+12   (implied)
  Row 12 -> T_i+16   (implied)
  Row 13 -> T_i+20   (implied)
  Row 14 -> T_i+24   (implied)
  Row 15 -> T_i+28   (bits [31..16] for cols 0-15)

The same thread T_i holds metadata for both row 0 (in bits [15..0]) and row 8 (in bits [31..16]).
The same thread T_i+4 holds metadata for both row 1 (in bits [15..0]) and row 9 (in bits [31..16]).
This pattern continues through T_i+28 covering rows 7 and 15.

==============================================================
5. COLUMN/ROW GROUPING PATTERNS
==============================================================

Column grouping: Columns are grouped in sets of 4 (cols 0-3, 4-7, 8-11, 12-15).
Each group of 4 columns corresponds to one 4-bit metadata field.
Within each 4-column group, the 2:4 sparsity rule applies: exactly 2 of the 4 elements are non-zero, and the 4-bit field encodes which 2 positions are selected.

Row grouping: Rows 0-7 form the upper half; rows 8-15 form the lower half.
The upper half uses the lower 16 bits of the thread's metadata register.
The lower half uses the upper 16 bits of the thread's metadata register.

Thread stride within each half is 4 (T_i, T_i+4, T_i+8, ..., T_i+28), covering 8 rows per half using 8 threads spaced 4 apart in thread index.

The arrows visible in the figure indicate the continuation of the pattern from rows 1/9 through rows 7/15, confirming the stride-4 thread assignment fills in the omitted intermediate rows (2-6 and 10-14).

  >
  >
  > Figure 119 Sparse MMA .m16n8k16 metadata layout for `.f16`/`.bf16` type.

###### 9.7.14.6.2.2. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k32` with `.f16` and `.bf16` types](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-16832-f16bf16)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k32` with `.f16` / `.bf16` floating point type will compute
an MMA operation of shape `.m16n8k32`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.f16` / `.bf16` | A vector expression containing four `.b32` registers, with each register containing two non-zero `.f16` / `.bf16` elements out of 4 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 120](#sparse-mma-16832-f16-bf16-a).

4. GROUPING PATTERNS
=============================================================
THREAD GROUPING (M dimension / rows):
- Rows 0-7:   Threads T0-T31 map in groups of 4 per row
  - Row 0: T0, T1, T2, T3

  - Row 2: T8, T9, T10, T11 (implied by pattern)

REGISTER ELEMENT GROUPING (K dimension / columns):

- Columns 16..31 (K positions 16-31): register elements a4, a5 (for rows 0-7) or a6, a7 (for rows 8-15)

=============================================================
Each thread holds 8 register elements total: a0, a1, a2, a3, a4, a5, a6, a7
Packed as pairs in 32-bit registers:
  - Register containing {a0, a1}: covers K columns 0..15, matrix rows 0..7
  - Register containing {a2, a3}: covers K columns 0..15, matrix rows 8..15
  - Register containing {a4, a5}: covers K columns 16..31, matrix rows 0..7
  - Register containing {a6, a7}: covers K columns 16..31, matrix rows 8..15

Each 32-bit register holds 2 f16 or bf16 values packed together.
Total registers per thread for matrix A: 4 registers (each holding 2 elements = 8 elements total).

=============================================================
6. COLUMN COVERAGE PER THREAD
=============================================================
Each thread covers 4 consecutive column positions within each column group (groups of 4 columns span 4 K-positions), and each thread appears in all 4 column positions within its group (columns 0..3, 4..7, 8..11, 12..15 all map to the same thread with the same register elements, indicating the 4 columns within a group correspond to the 4 threads in a row sharing the same register values — i.e., T0 appears at column 0, T1 at column 1, T2 at column 2, T3 at column 3 within the 0..3 group).

  Figure 120 Sparse MMA .m16n8k32 fragment layout for matrix A with `.f16`/`.bf16` type.

  The row and column of a matrix fragment can be computed as:

  ```
  groupID = %laneid >> 2
  threadID_in_group = %laneid % 4

  row =      groupID            for ai where  0 <= i < 2 || 4 <= i < 6
             groupID + 8        Otherwise

  col = [firstcol ... lastcol]  // As per the mapping of non-zero elements
                                // as described in Sparse matrix storage

  Where
  firstcol = threadID_in_group * 4          For ai where i <  4
             (threadID_in_group * 4) + 16   for ai where i >= 4
  lastcol  = firstcol + 3
  ```

==============================================================
4. METADATA ENCODING FORMAT
==============================================================
Each group of 4 columns (e.g., cols 0..3) corresponds to 4 consecutive K-dimension elements.
For 2:4 sparsity, exactly 2 of every 4 elements are non-zero.
Each 4-bit field encodes which 2 elements (out of 4) are the non-zero ones.

Bit field width per column group: 4 bits
Each 4-bit field selects 2 non-zero positions out of 4 possible positions (indices 0,1,2,3).
The 4 bits are split into two 2-bit selectors:
  - Lower 2 bits [1..0]: index (0-3) of the first non-zero element in the group of 4
  - Upper 2 bits [3..2]: index (0-3) of the second non-zero element in the group of 4

Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register:
  - Bits [15..0]:  metadata for rows 0-7 (upper band)
  - Bits [31..16]: metadata for rows 8-15 (lower band)

Within each 16-bit half:
  - Bits [3..0]:   column group 0..3
  - Bits [7..4]:   column group 4..7
  - Bits [11..8]:  column group 8..11
  - Bits [15..12]: column group 12..15

==============================================================
5. THREAD GROUPING AND INDEXING PATTERNS
==============================================================
The index variable i ranges such that thread pairs (T_{2i}, T_{2i+1}) cover columns 0..15 and 16..31 respectively for a given row.

Thread index progression across rows (upper band, rows 0-7):
  Row 0: threads T_{2i},   T_{2i+1}   (offset +0)
  Row 1: threads T_{2i+4}, T_{2i+5}   (offset +4)
  Row 2: threads T_{2i+8}, T_{2i+9}   (offset +8, implied)
  Row 3: threads T_{2i+12},T_{2i+13}  (offset +12, implied)
  Row 4: threads T_{2i+16},T_{2i+17}  (offset +16, implied)
  Row 5: threads T_{2i+20},T_{2i+21}  (offset +20, implied)
  Row 6: threads T_{2i+24},T_{2i+25}  (offset +24, implied)
  Row 7: threads T_{2i+28},T_{2i+29}  (offset +28)

The same thread index pattern repeats for the lower band (rows 8-15), but using the upper 16 bits [31..16] of the same threads' metadata registers.

Column grouping: 8 groups of 4 columns each (0..3, 4..7, 8..11, 12..15, 16..19, 20..23, 24..27, 28..31). The first 4 column groups (cols 0..15) are covered by even-indexed thread T_{2i+offset}, and the second 4 column groups (cols 16..31) are covered by odd-indexed thread T_{2i+offset+1}.

Each thread thus provides metadata for exactly one row and one half (left or right) of the 32 K-dimension columns, storing 4 bits per group-of-4 columns x 4 groups = 16 bits per row half, packed into a 32-bit register covering two rows (one from the upper band, one from the lower band).

  >
  >
  > Figure 122 Sparse MMA .m16n8k32 metadata layout for `.f16`/`.bf16` type.

###### 9.7.14.6.2.3. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k16` with `.tf32` floating point type](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-16816-tf32)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k16` with `.tf32` floating point type will compute an MMA
operation of shape `.m16n8k16`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.tf32` | A vector expression containing four `.b32` registers, with each register containing one non-zero `.tf32` element out of 2 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 123](#sparse-mma-16816-tf32-a).

  Figure 123 Sparse MMA .m16n8k16 fragment layout for matrix A with `.tf32` type.

  The row and column of a matrix fragment can be computed as:

  ```
  groupID = %laneid >> 2
  threadID_in_group = %laneid % 4

  row =      groupID            for a0 and a2
             groupID + 8        for a1 and a3

  col = [firstcol ... lastcol]  // As per the mapping of non-zero elements
                                // as described in Sparse matrix storage

  Where
  firstcol = threadID_in_group * 2          for a0 and a1
             (threadID_in_group * 2) + 8    for a2 and a3
  lastcol  = firstcol + 1
  ```
* Multiplicand B:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements (low to high) |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.tf32` | A vector expression containing four `.b32` registers, each containing four `.tf32` elements from matrix B. | b0, b1, b2, b3 |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 124](#sparse-mma-16816-tf32-b).

MMA .m16n8k16 Fragment Layout for Matrix B with .f64 Type

1. MATRIX DIMENSIONS AND IDENTITY
Operation: MMA m16n8k16
Matrix: B (input matrix)
Data type: .f64 (64-bit double precision floating point)
Matrix B logical dimensions: 16 rows x 8 columns (K=16 rows, N=8 columns)

2. GRID STRUCTURE
The table has:
Rows (labeled 0-15): represent the 16 rows of matrix B (K dimension)
Columns (labeled 0, 1, 2, ..., 7): represent the 8 columns of matrix B (N dimension)
Entries: ThreadID:RegisterFragment notation (e.g., T0:B0 means Thread 0, register fragment B0)

3. COMPLETE THREAD-TO-ELEMENT MAPPING

The table shows columns 0, 1, and 7 explicitly. Columns 2 through 6 are implied by the downward arrows (continuation pattern).

ROWS 0-3 (first group, register B0):
Row 0: Col 0 = T0:B0,  Col 1 = T4:B0,  Col 7 = T28:B0
Row 1: Col 0 = T1:B0,  Col 1 = T5:B0,  Col 7 = T29:B0
Row 2: Col 0 = T2:B0,  Col 1 = T6:B0,  Col 7 = T30:B0
Row 3: Col 0 = T3:B0,  Col 1 = T7:B0,  Col 7 = T31:B0

ROWS 4-7 (second group, register B1):
Row 4: Col 0 = T0:B1,  Col 1 = T4:B1,  Col 7 = T28:B1
Row 5: Col 0 = T1:B1,  Col 1 = T5:B1,  Col 7 = T29:B1
Row 6: Col 0 = T2:B1,  Col 1 = T6:B1,  Col 7 = T30:B1
Row 7: Col 0 = T3:B1,  Col 1 = T7:B1,  Col 7 = T31:B1

ROWS 8-11 (third group, register B2):
Row 8:  Col 0 = T0:B2,  Col 1 = T4:B2,  Col 7 = T28:B2
Row 9:  Col 0 = T1:B2,  Col 1 = T5:B2,  Col 7 = T29:B2
Row 10: Col 0 = T2:B2,  Col 1 = T6:B2,  Col 7 = T30:B2
Row 11: Col 0 = T3:B2,  Col 1 = T7:B2,  Col 7 = T31:B2

ROWS 12-15 (fourth group, register B3):
Row 12: Col 0 = T0:B3,  Col 1 = T4:B3,  Col 7 = T28:B3
Row 13: Col 0 = T1:B3,  Col 1 = T5:B3,  Col 7 = T29:B3
Row 14: Col 0 = T2:B3,  Col 1 = T6:B3,  Col 7 = T30:B3
Row 15: Col 0 = T3:B3,  Col 1 = T7:B3,  Col 7 = T31:B3

4. DATA TYPE
.f64 = 64-bit double precision floating point
Each register fragment (B0, B1, B2, B3) holds one f64 value (one 64-bit element), stored as a pair of 32-bit registers in PTX (since PTX registers are 32-bit, each f64 occupies two consecutive 32-bit registers).

5. GROUPING PATTERNS

Column grouping (N dimension, 8 columns total):
Columns 0-7 are distributed across threads T0-T31 in groups of 4 threads per column-pair.
Each group of 4 consecutive threads (T0-T3, T4-T7, T8-T11, ..., T28-T31) covers one column of matrix B.
Specifically:
  Column 0: threads T0, T1, T2, T3 (rows 0,1,2,3 respectively within each row-group)
  Column 1: threads T4, T5, T6, T7
  Column 2: threads T8, T9, T10, T11
  Column 3: threads T12, T13, T14, T15
  Column 4: threads T16, T17, T18, T19
  Column 5: threads T20, T21, T22, T23
  Column 6: threads T24, T25, T26, T27
  Column 7: threads T28, T29, T30, T31

Row grouping (K dimension, 16 rows total):
Rows 0-3   map to register B0 for all threads
Rows 4-7   map to register B1 for all threads
Rows 8-11  map to register B2 for all threads
Rows 12-15 map to register B3 for all threads

Each thread holds 4 register fragments: B0, B1, B2, B3.

6. REGISTER PACKING
Data type is .f64 (64-bit). In PTX, f64 values require 64 bits of storage.
Each fragment Bn corresponds to one f64 element, stored in a 64-bit register (or equivalently two 32-bit registers as a register pair in PTX notation).
There is NO packing of multiple values into a single register (unlike f16 where two values share one 32-bit register). Each f64 element occupies its own 64-bit register fragment.
Each thread holds exactly 4 f64 register fragments: B0, B1, B2, B3, covering one column of matrix B across all 16 K-rows.

SUMMARY PER THREAD:
Every thread Tx (where x = 0 to 31) holds:
  B0: element at matrix B[row_group_0][col_x] where col_x = floor(x/4), row within group = x mod 4, rows 0-3
  B1: element at matrix B[row_group_1][col_x], rows 4-7
  B2: element at matrix B[row_group_2][col_x], rows 8-11
  B3: element at matrix B[row_group_3][col_x], rows 12-15

The label at the bottom "%laneid:{fragments}" confirms that the lane ID (thread index within warp, 0-31) determines which column and which row-within-group each thread owns.

ROWS 10-14: Follow the same pattern as rows 8-9, continuing with thread offsets incrementing by 4 per two rows (implied by diagonal arrows).

ROW 15:
  Cols 0-1:   T_{2i+28}: [19..16]
  Cols 2-3:   T_{2i+28}: [23..20]
  Cols 4-5:   T_{2i+28}: [27..24]
  Cols 6-7:   T_{2i+28}: [31..28]
  Cols 8-9:   T_{2i+29}: [19..16]
  Cols 10-11: T_{2i+29}: [23..20]
  Cols 12-13: T_{2i+29}: [27..24]
  Cols 14-15: T_{2i+29}: [31..28]

=============================================================
4. METADATA ENCODING FORMAT
=============================================================
Each cell contains 4 bits of metadata (e.g., [3..0], [7..4], [11..8], [15..12] for upper half; [19..16], [23..20], [27..24], [31..28] for lower half).

For .tf32 type with 2:4 sparsity:
- Each group of 4 consecutive elements in a row contains exactly 2 non-zero values.
- 4 bits encode the positions of the 2 non-zero elements out of 4.
- Each pair of bits selects one non-zero element position (values 0-3 indicating which of the 4 positions is non-zero).
- Bits [1..0] select the first non-zero element position (0, 1, 2, or 3).
- Bits [3..2] select the second non-zero element position (0, 1, 2, or 3).

=============================================================
5. COLUMN AND ROW GROUPING PATTERNS
=============================================================
Column grouping:
- Columns 0-7 are covered by thread T_{2i} (or T_{2i+offset} for lower rows).
- Columns 8-15 are covered by thread T_{2i+1} (or T_{2i+1+offset} for lower rows).
- Within each 8-column half, every 2 columns correspond to a 4-bit field within the 32-bit thread register:
    Cols 0-1 (or 8-9):   bits [3..0]   (upper rows) / [19..16] (lower rows)
    Cols 2-3 (or 10-11): bits [7..4]   (upper rows) / [23..20] (lower rows)
    Cols 4-5 (or 12-13): bits [11..8]  (upper rows) / [27..24] (lower rows)
    Cols 6-7 (or 14-15): bits [15..12] (upper rows) / [31..28] (lower rows)

Row grouping:
- Rows 0-7 (upper half): use bits [15..0] of the thread's 32-bit metadata register.
- Rows 8-15 (lower half): use bits [31..16] of the thread's 32-bit metadata register.
- Within each half, every 2 rows share the same thread index offset pattern, advancing by +4 in thread index per 2 rows:
    Rows 0,8:   thread offset 0 (T_{2i}, T_{2i+1})
    Rows 1,9:   thread offset 4 (T_{2i+4}, T_{2i+5})
    Rows 2,10:  thread offset 8 (T_{2i+8}, T_{2i+9})
    Rows 3,11:  thread offset 12 (T_{2i+12}, T_{2i+13})
    Rows 4,12:  thread offset 16 (T_{2i+16}, T_{2i+17})
    Rows 5,13:  thread offset 20 (T_{2i+20}, T_{2i+21})
    Rows 6,14:  thread offset 24 (T_{2i+24}, T_{2i+25})
    Rows 7,15:  thread offset 28 (T_{2i+28}, T_{2i+29})

The parameter i ranges over values such that all 32 threads in a warp are covered (i = 0, 1, 2, 3 giving thread pairs 0-1, 2-3, 4-5, 6-7 etc., up to thread 31).

  >
  > Figure 125 Sparse MMA .m16n8k16 metadata layout for `.tf32` type.

###### 9.7.14.6.2.4. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k8` with `.tf32` floating point type](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-1688-tf32)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k8` with `.tf32` floating point type will compute an MMA
operation of shape `.m16n8k8`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.tf32` | A vector expression containing two `.b32` registers, each containing one non-zero `.tf32` element out of 2 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 126](#sparse-mma-1688-tf32).

GROUPING PATTERNS:
- Rows 0-7 (upper half of M dimension): all 32 threads hold register element a0
- Rows 8-15 (lower half of M dimension): all 32 threads hold register element a1
- Each group of 4 consecutive threads (T0-T3, T4-T7, ..., T28-T31) covers one full row across all 8 columns
- Within each row, each thread covers a 2-column span
- Thread index within a row group determines column pair: thread%4 * 2 = starting column
- Row within upper/lower half determined by: floor(thread_id / 4)

REGISTER PACKING:
- Data type is .tf32 (19-bit precision stored in 32-bit register)
- One tf32 value per 32-bit register (no packing of multiple values)
- Each thread holds exactly 2 registers for matrix A: a0 and a1
- a0 holds the element from the corresponding row in the upper half (rows 0-7)
- a1 holds the element from the corresponding row in the lower half (rows 8-15)
- Total registers per thread for matrix A: 2 x 32-bit registers


  Figure 126 Sparse MMA .m16n8k8 fragment layout for matrix A with `.tf32` type.

  The row and column of a matrix fragment can be computed as:

  ```
  groupID = %laneid >> 2
  threadID_in_group = %laneid % 4

  row =      groupID            for a0
             groupID + 8        for a1

  col = [firstcol ... lastcol]  // As per the mapping of non-zero elements
                                // as described in Sparse matrix storage

  Where
  firstcol = threadID_in_group * 2
  lastcol  = firstcol + 1
  ```
* Matrix fragments for multiplicand B and accumulators C and D are the same as in case of
  [Matrix Fragments for mma.m16n8k8](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-mma-1688) for `.tf32`
  format.
* Metadata: A `.b32` register containing 8 4-bit vectors each storing the index of a non-zero
  element of a 2-wide chunk of matrix A as shown in [Figure 127](#sparse-mma-metadata-1688-tf32).

=============================================================
1. MMA SHAPE AND DATA TYPE
=============================================================
Shape: m16n8k8
Data type: .tf32
Matrix A is 16 rows x 8 columns (sparse, 2:4 structured sparsity)
Metadata encodes which 2 of every 4 elements are non-zero

=============================================================
2. TABLE STRUCTURE: Row\Column Layout
=============================================================
The table has rows 0-15 (matrix rows) and columns 0-7 (grouped in pairs: 0-1, 2-3, 4-5, 6-7).

Columns 0-1 share one cell, columns 2-3 share one cell, columns 4-5 share one cell, columns 6-7 share one cell. This means metadata covers pairs of columns together.

=============================================================
3. COMPLETE THREAD AND BIT ASSIGNMENT TABLE
=============================================================

UPPER HALF (Rows 0-7):

Row 0:
  Columns 0-1: Ti:   bits [3..0]
  Columns 2-3: Ti:   bits [7..4]
  Columns 4-5: Ti:   bits [11..8]
  Columns 6-7: Ti:   bits [15..12]

Row 1:
  Columns 0-1: Ti+4:  bits [3..0]
  Columns 2-3: Ti+4:  bits [7..4]
  Columns 4-5: Ti+4:  bits [11..8]
  Columns 6-7: Ti+4:  bits [15..12]

Row 2:
  (empty cells — pattern continues via arrows)

Row ... (rows 3-6 implied by arrows):
  (continuation of pattern)

Row 7:
  Columns 0-1: Ti+28: bits [3..0]
  Columns 2-3: Ti+28: bits [7..4]
  Columns 4-5: Ti+28: bits [11..8]
  Columns 6-7: Ti+28: bits [15..12]

LOWER HALF (Rows 8-15):

Row 8:
  Columns 0-1: Ti:    bits [19..16]
  Columns 2-3: Ti:    bits [23..20]
  Columns 4-5: Ti:    bits [27..24]
  Columns 6-7: Ti:    bits [31..28]

Row 9:
  Columns 0-1: Ti+4:  bits [19..16]
  Columns 2-3: Ti+4:  bits [23..20]
  Columns 4-5: Ti+4:  bits [27..24]
  Columns 6-7: Ti+4:  bits [31..28]

Row 10:
  (empty cells — pattern continues via arrows)

Row ... (rows 11-14 implied by arrows):
  (continuation of pattern)

Row 15:
  Columns 0-1: Ti+28: bits [19..16]
  Columns 2-3: Ti+28: bits [23..20]
  Columns 4-5: Ti+28: bits [27..24]
  Columns 6-7: Ti+28: bits [31..28]

=============================================================
4. THREAD INDEXING PATTERN
=============================================================
- Base thread index is Ti, where i is the starting thread in a group.
- Rows 0-7 use threads Ti, Ti+4, Ti+8, ..., Ti+28 (stepping by 4 per row).
- Rows 8-15 use the same threads Ti, Ti+4, Ti+8, ..., Ti+28 but use the upper 16 bits [31..16] of the metadata register.
- Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register.
- The arrows in the figure indicate that rows 2 through 6 (and 10 through 14) follow the same pattern as rows 0,1 and 7, with thread indices incrementing by 4 per row step.

=============================================================
5. METADATA REGISTER BIT ALLOCATION PER THREAD
=============================================================
Each thread Ti holds a single 32-bit metadata value split as follows:

Bits [15..0]  — encode metadata for rows 0-7 (upper matrix half):
  bits [3..0]   -> columns 0-1 of the thread's assigned row in rows 0-7
  bits [7..4]   -> columns 2-3 of the thread's assigned row in rows 0-7
  bits [11..8]  -> columns 4-5 of the thread's assigned row in rows 0-7
  bits [15..12] -> columns 6-7 of the thread's assigned row in rows 0-7

Bits [31..16] — encode metadata for rows 8-15 (lower matrix half):
  bits [19..16] -> columns 0-1 of the thread's assigned row in rows 8-15
  bits [23..20] -> columns 2-3 of the thread's assigned row in rows 8-15
  bits [27..24] -> columns 4-5 of the thread's assigned row in rows 8-15
  bits [31..28] -> columns 6-7 of the thread's assigned row in rows 8-15

=============================================================
6. METADATA ENCODING FORMAT
=============================================================
For .tf32 with 2:4 sparsity, each group of 4 elements has exactly 2 non-zero values. Each pair of columns (e.g., columns 0-1) corresponds to one 4-element group in the K dimension. The 4-bit field selects which 2 of the 4 elements are non-zero:
  - 2 bits select the position of the first non-zero element (values 0-3)
  - 2 bits select the position of the second non-zero element (values 0-3)
This is consistent with standard NVIDIA 2:4 sparsity metadata encoding where each 4-bit field encodes two 2-bit indices into the dense group of 4.

=============================================================
7. COLUMN GROUPING PATTERN
=============================================================
Columns are grouped in pairs: {0,1}, {2,3}, {4,5}, {6,7}.
Each pair of columns corresponds to one 4-bit metadata field within the thread's 32-bit register.
There are 4 column groups x 4 bits = 16 bits used per matrix half (rows 0-7 or rows 8-15).
Total: 32 bits per thread register fully utilized.

=============================================================
8. ARROW ANNOTATIONS
=============================================================
Two sets of diagonal arrows appear in the figure:
- In the upper half (rows 0-7): arrows indicate the pattern from row 1 (Ti+4) continues through rows 2-6, incrementing thread index by 4 per row, until row 7 (Ti+28).
- In the lower half (rows 8-15): arrows indicate the same continuation from row 9 (Ti+4) through rows 10-14, until row 15 (Ti+28).
The arrows visually connect the explicit entries at rows 1 and 7 (and 9 and 15) to show the implicit rows follow the same thread-stepping pattern.

###### 9.7.14.6.2.5. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k32` with `.u8` / `.s8` integer type](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-16832-u8s8)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k32` with `.u8` / `.s8` integer type will compute an MMA
operation of shape `.m16n8k32`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.u8` / `.s8` | A vector expression containing two `.b32` registers, with each register containing four non-zero `.u8` / `.s8` elements out of 8 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 128](#sparse-mma-16832-u8s8-a).

   Row 15, Columns 24..31:  T31: {a4, a5, a6, a7}

4. GROUPING PATTERN SUMMARY
   Matrix rows 0..7 are covered by threads T0..T31, each thread covering one 8-column segment of one row, holding elements {a0,a1,a2,a3}.
   Matrix rows 8..15 are covered by the same threads T0..T31 in the same column arrangement, holding elements {a4,a5,a6,a7}.
   Within rows 0..7: each row is divided into 4 column groups (0..7, 8..15, 16..23, 24..31), assigned to 4 consecutive threads. Row r uses threads T(4r)..T(4r+3).
   Within rows 8..15: row r (r in 8..15) uses threads T(4*(r-8))..T(4*(r-8)+3), same thread IDs as the corresponding row in the first half, but different register elements.

5. REGISTER PACKING
   Data type is 8-bit (.u8 or .s8).
   Each 32-bit register holds 4 x 8-bit values.
   Therefore {a0,a1,a2,a3} are packed into one 32-bit register (register 0 of the fragment).
   {a4,a5,a6,a7} are packed into a second 32-bit register (register 1 of the fragment).
   Each thread holds 2 x 32-bit registers total for matrix A, containing 8 x 8-bit elements.
   Total elements per thread: 8 (4 per register, 2 registers).
   This is the sparse (compressed) representation; the logical K=32 is compressed to physical K=16 due to 2:4 structured sparsity, so each thread stores 8 non-zero 8-bit values.


  Figure 128 Sparse MMA .m16n8k32 fragment layout for matrix A with `.u8`/`.s8` type.

  ```
  groupID = %laneid >> 2
  threadID_in_group = %laneid % 4

  row =      groupID            for ai where  0 <= i < 4
             groupID + 8        Otherwise

  col = [firstcol ... lastcol]  // As per the mapping of non-zero elements
                                // as described in Sparse matrix storage

  Where
  firstcol = threadID_in_group * 8
  lastcol  = firstcol + 7
  ```
* Matrix fragments for multiplicand B and accumulators C and D are the same as in case of
  [Matrix Fragments for mma.m16n8k32](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-mma-16832).
* Metadata: A `.b32` register containing 16 2-bit vectors with each pair of 2-bit vectors storing
  the indices of two non-zero elements from a 4-wide chunk of matrix A as shown in
  [Figure 129](#sparse-mma-metadata-16832-u8s8).

==============================================================
4. THREAD INDEXING PATTERN
==============================================================
The variable i ranges such that thread indices cover the warp (32 threads).
The thread subscript formula follows a structured pattern:

For rows 0..7 (upper half of M):
  Row 0:  thread T_{2i},      all 8 column groups use bits [3..0] through [31..28]
  Row 1:  thread T_{2i+4}
  Row 2:  thread T_{2i+8}     (implied by pattern)
  Row 3:  thread T_{2i+12}    (implied)
  Row 4:  thread T_{2i+16}    (implied)
  Row 5:  thread T_{2i+20}    (implied)
  Row 6:  thread T_{2i+24}    (implied)
  Row 7:  thread T_{2i+28}

For rows 8..15 (lower half of M):
  Row 8:  thread T_{2i+1}
  Row 9:  thread T_{2i+5}
  Row 10: thread T_{2i+9}     (implied)
  Row 11: thread T_{2i+13}    (implied)
  Row 12: thread T_{2i+17}    (implied)
  Row 13: thread T_{2i+21}    (implied)
  Row 14: thread T_{2i+25}    (implied)
  Row 15: thread T_{2i+29}

The upper half (rows 0..7) uses even-based thread offsets (2i, 2i+4, 2i+8, ..., 2i+28).
The lower half (rows 8..15) uses odd-based thread offsets (2i+1, 2i+5, 2i+9, ..., 2i+29).
The step between consecutive rows within each half is +4 in the thread offset.

==============================================================
5. BIT FIELD ASSIGNMENTS PER COLUMN GROUP
==============================================================
Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register. The bits are partitioned into
8 groups of 4 bits each, one group per column range:

  Column group 0..3:   bits [3..0]   (bits 3 down to 0)
  Column group 4..7:   bits [7..4]   (bits 7 down to 4)
  Column group 8..11:  bits [11..8]  (bits 11 down to 8)
  Column group 12..15: bits [15..12] (bits 15 down to 12)
  Column group 16..19: bits [19..16] (bits 19 down to 16)
  Column group 20..23: bits [23..20] (bits 23 down to 20)
  Column group 24..27: bits [27..24] (bits 27 down to 24)
  Column group 28..31: bits [31..28] (bits 31 down to 28)

Each 4-bit field encodes the sparsity metadata for 4 consecutive K-dimension elements
(one column group of 4 columns). For 2:4 sparsity, 2 bits select which 2 of the 4
elements are non-zero, requiring 2 bits per pair. The 4-bit field covers one group of
4 elements, encoding two 2-bit selectors (one per non-zero element position out of 4).

==============================================================
6. METADATA ENCODING SEMANTICS
==============================================================
For .u8/.s8 with k=32:
- The sparse A matrix has 2 non-zero values for every 4 values along the K dimension.
- Each 4-bit metadata chunk (per column group of 4) encodes which 2 of the 4 elements
  are non-zero using two 2-bit indices.
- Bits [1..0] of each 4-bit field: index of the first non-zero element (0, 1, 2, or 3)
- Bits [3..2] of each 4-bit field: index of the second non-zero element (0, 1, 2, or 3)
- This allows reconstruction of which columns within each group of 4 contain the
  non-zero values that are stored in the compressed sparse A fragment.

==============================================================
7. DIAGONAL ARROWS IN FIGURE
==============================================================
Two diagonal arrows appear in the figure:
- First arrow: spans from approximately row 1 / column 28..31 diagonally down-left
  to row 7 / column 0..3, indicating that

###### 9.7.14.6.2.6. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k64` with `.u8` / `.s8` / `.e4m3` / `.e5m2` type](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-16864-u8s8-fp8)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k64` with `.u8` / `.s8`/ `.e4m3`/ `.e5m2` /
`.e3m2` / `.e2m3` / `.e2m1` type will compute an MMA operation of shape `.m16n8k64`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.u8` / `.s8` | A vector expression containing four `.b32` registers, with each register containing four non-zero `.u8` / `.s8` elements out of 8 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |
  | `.e4m3` / `.e5m2` / `.e3m2` / `.e2m3` / `.e2m1` | A vector expression containing four `.b32` registers, with each register containing four non-zero `.e4m3` / `.e5m2` / `.e3m2` / `.e2m3` / `.e2m1` elements out of 8 consecutive elements from matrix A. |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 130](#sparse-mma-16864-u8s8-a-first32col)
  and [Figure 131](#sparse-mma-16864-u8s8-a-last32col).

4. GROUPING PATTERNS
---------------------
Thread assignment pattern: Each matrix row is covered by 4 threads (one per 8-column group).
Rows 0-7 map to threads T0-T31 in groups of 4 per row:
  Row 0 -> T0, T1, T2, T3
  Row 1 -> T4, T5, T6, T7
  Row 2 -> T8, T9, T10, T11
  Row 3 -> T12, T13, T14, T15
  Row 4 -> T16, T17, T18, T19
  Row 5 -> T20, T21, T22, T23
  Row 6 -> T24, T25, T26, T27
  Row 7 -> T28, T29, T30, T31

Rows 8-15 reuse the SAME threads T0-T31 in the same grouping pattern, but use register elements a4-a7 instead of a0-a3.

5. REGISTER PACKING
--------------------
Each thread holds 8 register elements total for this fragment: a0, a1, a2, a3, a4, a5, a6, a7.
Elements a0-a3 cover matrix rows 0-7 (columns 0-31).
Elements a4-a7 cover matrix rows 8-15 (columns 0-31).
For 8-bit types (u8/s8) and floating-point 8-bit types (e4m3/e5m2/e3m2/e2m3/e2m1): four 8-bit values are packed into each 32-bit register, so {a0,a1,a2,a3} occupies one 32-bit register and {a4,a5,a6,a7} occupies one 32-bit register, giving 2 registers per thread total for this fragment covering columns 0-31.
Each thread covers 8 consecutive columns within its assigned column group (spanning 8 columns out of the 32 shown).

  ```
  groupID = %laneid >> 2
  threadID_in_group = %laneid % 4

  row =      groupID            for ai where  0 <= i < 4 || 8 <= i < 12
             groupID + 8        Otherwise

  col = [firstcol ... lastcol]  // As per the mapping of non-zero elements
                                // as described in Sparse matrix storage

  Where
  firstcol = threadID_in_group * 8           For ai where i <  8
             (threadID_in_group * 8) + 32    For ai where i >= 8
  lastcol  = firstcol + 7
  ```

Rows (matrix rows 32-47) are divided into 4 groups of 4 rows each:
  Row group 1: rows 32-35
  Row group 2: rows 36-39
  Row group 3: rows 40-43
  Row group 4: rows 44-47

Columns: 0 through 7 (8 columns)

=== THREAD-TO-ELEMENT MAPPING ===

Each thread holds 4 register elements: b8, b9, b10, b11 (packed in registers)

ROW GROUP: rows 32-35
  Column 0: T0  holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}
  Column 1: T4  holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}
  Column 2: (continues pattern, arrow indicates progression)
  ...
  Column 7: T28 holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}

ROW GROUP: rows 36-39
  Column 0: T1  holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}
  Column 1: T5  holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}
  Column 2: (continues pattern)
  ...
  Column 7: T29 holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}

ROW GROUP: rows 40-43
  Column 0: T2  holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}
  Column 1: T6  holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}
  Column 2: (continues pattern)
  ...
  Column 7: T30 holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}

ROW GROUP: rows 44-47
  Column 0: T3  holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}
  Column 1: T7  holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}
  Column 2: (continues pattern)
  ...
  Column 7: T31 holds {b8, b9, b10, b11}

=== THREAD ASSIGNMENT PATTERN ===
Thread ID within each column group follows this pattern:
  Column 0: T0, T1, T2, T3  (for row groups 32-35, 36-39, 40-43, 44-47 respectively)
  Column 1: T4, T5, T6, T7
  Column 2: T8, T9, T10, T11
  Column 3: T12, T13, T14, T15
  Column 4: T16, T17, T18, T19
  Column 5: T20, T21, T22, T23
  Column 6: T24, T25, T26, T27
  Column 7: T28, T29, T30, T31

General formula: Thread = (column_index * 4) + row_group_index
  where row_group_index: 0=rows32-35, 1=rows36-39, 2=rows40-43, 3=rows44-47

=== REGISTER ELEMENT DETAILS ===
Each thread stores 4 elements labeled b8, b9, b10, b11
These are the SECOND set of 4 register elements (b0-b7 appear in rows 0-31, b8-b11 appear in rows 32-47)
For 8-bit data types (u8/s8) or floating point types (e4m3/e5m2/e3m2/e2m3/e2m1):
  Multiple 8-bit elements are packed into each 32-bit register
  b8 and b9 are packed into one 32-bit register
  b10 and b11 are packed into one 32-bit register
  (4 elements total per thread = 2 registers of 32 bits each, with 2 elements per register for 8-bit types)

=== TOTAL THREAD COVERAGE ===
All 32 threads (T0-T31) are used
Each thread covers one specific (row-group, column) intersection
32 threads x 4 elements = 128 element slots covering the 16-row x 8-column sparse B fragment for rows 32-47

THREAD ASSIGNMENT PATTERN

Rows 0–7 use threads T0, T4, T8, T12, T16, T20, T24, T28 (stride of +4 per row).
  Row 0 -> T0
  Row 1 -> T4
  Row 2 -> T8
  Row 3 -> T12
  Row 4 -> T16
  Row 5 -> T20
  Row 6 -> T24
  Row 7 -> T28

Rows 8–15 use threads T1, T5, T9, T13, T17, T21, T25, T29 (stride of +4 per row, offset +1 from upper half).
  Row 8  -> T1
  Row 9  -> T5
  Row 10 -> T9
  Row 11 -> T13
  Row 12 -> T17
  Row 13 -> T21
  Row 14 -> T25
  Row 15 -> T29

BIT FIELD ASSIGNMENT PATTERN

Each thread holds a single 32-bit metadata register covering all 8 column groups (cols 0..31).
Each group of 4 columns consumes exactly 4 bits of the metadata register.
The bit fields are contiguous and non-overlapping across the 32-bit register:

  Cols 0..3:   bits [3..0]
  Cols 4..7:   bits [7..4]
  Cols 8..11:  bits [11..8]
  Cols 12..15: bits [15..12]
  Cols 16..19: bits [19..16]
  Cols 20..23: bits [23..20]
  Cols 24..27: bits [27..24]
  Cols 28..31: bits [31..28]

This means each 4-bit field encodes the sparsity selector for 4 consecutive columns within one matrix row. For 2:4 structured sparsity, each group of 4 elements contains exactly 2 non-zeros; 4 bits encode two 2-bit selectors (each 2-bit value selects which of the 4 positions is non-zero).

DIAGONAL ARROWS
Two diagonal arrows are shown in the figure:
1. An arrow from the row 1 / cols 28..31 cell (T4: [31..28]) pointing diagonally down-left toward row 7 / cols 0..3 (T28: [3..0]), indicating the continuation of the thread sequence T4 -> T8 -> ... -> T28 across rows 2–7.
2. An arrow from the row 9 / cols 28..31 cell (T5: [31..28]) pointing diagonally down-left toward row 15 / cols 0..3 (T29: [3..0]), indicating the continuation of the thread sequence T5 -> T9 -> ... -> T29 across rows 10–15.

SUMMARY OF KEY STRUCTURAL RULES
1. Each matrix row maps to exactly one thread.
2. Each thread's 32-bit register covers all 32 columns (cols 0..31) for that row.
3. Bit fields are 4 bits wide per 4-column group, packed LSB to MSB left to right.
4. Upper 8 rows (0–7) use even-indexed threads in multiples of 4: T0, T4, T8, T12, T16, T20, T24, T28.
5. Lower 8 rows (8–15) use odd-indexed threads offset by 1: T1, T5, T9, T13, T17, T21, T25, T29.
6. Threads T2, T3, T6, T7, T10, T11, etc. (those with index mod 4 = 2 or 3) are not used for this metadata layout in columns 0–31 (they likely cover columns 32–63, the second half of k=64).

THREAD PROGRESSION PATTERN:
Rows 0-7 use threads: T2, T6, ..., T30 (even-numbered threads starting at 2, incrementing by 4: T2, T6, T10, T14, T18, T22, T26, T30)
Rows 8-15 use threads: T3, T7, ..., T31 (odd-numbered threads starting at 3, incrementing by 4: T3, T7, T11, T15, T19, T23, T27, T31)

BIT ALLOCATION PER THREAD:
Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register [31..0].
The 32 bits are divided into 8 groups of 4 bits each:
  Bits [3..0]   -> column group 35..32
  Bits [7..4]   -> column group 39..36
  Bits [11..8]  -> column group 43..40
  Bits [15..12] -> column group 47..44
  Bits [19..16] -> column group 51..48
  Bits [23..20] -> column group 55..52
  Bits [27..24] -> column group 59..56
  Bits [31..28] -> column group 63..60

ENCODING FORMAT:
Each 4-bit field encodes sparsity metadata for 4 consecutive columns. In 2:4 structured sparsity, 2 non-zero elements are selected out of every 4 elements. Each 4-bit group encodes which 2 of the 4 elements in that column group are non-zero (2 bits per selected element index).

###### 9.7.14.6.2.7. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k64` with `.u4` / `.s4` integer type](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-16864-u4s4)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k64` with `.u4` / `.s4` integer type will compute an MMA
operation of shape `.m16n8k64`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.u4` / `.s4` | A vector expression containing two `.b32` registers, with each register containing eight non-zero `.u4` / `.s4` elements out of 16 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 138](#sparse-mma-16864-u4s4-a).

  - Row 13: T20, T21, T22, T23
  - Row 14: T24, T25, T26, T27
  - Row 15: T28, T29, T30, T31

COLUMN GROUPING:
- Each thread covers a 16-column span of the K dimension
- 4 threads per row, each covering 16 columns: columns 0-15, 16-31, 32-47, 48-63

==============================================================
5. REGISTER ELEMENT PACKING
==============================================================
Total elements per thread: 16 elements (a0 through a15)
Split into two groups:
- a0...a7:  8 elements, used for matrix rows 0-7
- a8...a15: 8 elements, used for matrix rows 8-15

Since the data type is 4-bit (.u4 or .s4):
- Each 32-bit register holds 8 x 4-bit values
- Therefore a0...a7 fits in 1 x 32-bit register
- And a8...a15 fits in 1 x 32-bit register
- Total: 2 x 32-bit registers per thread for the full matrix A fragment

Each thread covers 16 columns of K (one 16-column group) across 8 rows (either rows 0-7 or rows 8-15), with 8 four-bit elements packed per 32-bit register.

==============================================================
6. ARROWS IN FIGURE
==============================================================
Two diagonal arrows are shown in the figure:
- First arrow: spans from approximately (row 1, col 32-47) diagonally to (row 7, col 0-15), indicating the continuation of the thread pattern (T8 through T27) filling rows 2-6 implicitly.
- Second arrow: spans from approximately (row 9, col 32-47) diagonally to (row 15, col 0-15), indicating the same continuation for the second row block (rows 10-14, threads T8-T27 with a8...a15).

  ```
  groupID = %laneid >> 2
  threadID_in_group = %laneid % 4

  row =      groupID            for ai where  0 <= i < 8
             groupID + 8        Otherwise

  col = [firstcol ... lastcol]  // As per the mapping of non-zero elements
                                // as described in Sparse matrix storage

  Where
  firstcol = threadID_in_group * 16
  lastcol  = firstcol + 15
  ```
